elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we prove several new infinite families of Ramanujan--like congruences satisfied by the coefficients of the generating function $U_t(a,q)$ which is an extension of MacMahon's generalized sum-of-divisors function.
elements.sent-2411-002: As a by-product, we also show that, for all $n\geq 0$, $\B3(15n+7)\equiv 0 \pmod{5}$ where $\B3(n)$ is the number of almost $3$-regular overpartitions of $n$.
elements.sent-2411-003: In \cite{AAT2 , Amdeberhan, Andrews, and Tauraso introduced the generating function \begin{equation}\label{defofU} U_t(a,q):=\sum_{1\leq n_1<n_2<\cdots<n_t}\frac{q^{n_1+n_2+\cdots+n_t}}{\prod_{k=1}^t(1+aq^{n_k}+q^{2n_k})}=\sum_{n\geq0} MO(a,t;n)q^n.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-004: The particular case $U_t(-2,q)$ was initially studied by P.. A.
elements.sent-2411-005: MacMahon \cite{MacMahon as a generalization of the sum-of-divisors function, \begin{equation}U_1(-2,q)=\sum_{n\geq 1} \frac{q^n}{(1-q^n)^2}=\sum_{n\geq 1} \sigma(n) q^n.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-006: The function $U_t(-2,q)$ has received much attention in recent years; see \cite{AAT1, AOS, Andrews-Rose, OS for more details about the results that have already been proven.}
elements.sent-2411-007: In \cite{AAT2 , the authors investigated $U_t(a;q)$ for the other cases when the roots of $1+ax+x^2=0$ are also roots of unity.}
elements.sent-2411-009: As such, in this note we will continue the study of $U_t(a,q)$ for this set of values of $a$, focusing our attention on arithmetic properties satisfied by the coefficients $MO(a,t;n)$.
elements.sent-2411-010: From Theorem 2.1 and Appendix 2 in \cite{AAT2 , we have that $U_t(a,q)$ can be written as the product of two generating functions: \begin{equation}\label{idofU} U_t(a,q)=\sum_{k\geq 0} b_k(a) q^k \cdot \sum_{n\geq 0} c_{n}(a,t) q^{\binom{n+1}{2}}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\sum_{k\geq 0} b_k(a) q^k=\prod_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(1+aq^n+q^{2n})(1-q^n)}\end{equation} and \begin{equation}c_n(a,t)=(-1)^{n-t}[z^n]\frac{z^t(1+z)}{(1+az+z^2)^{t+1}}\end{equation} where $[z^n]\left( \sum_{k\geq 0 h_kq^k \right) = h_n$, the coefficient of $z^n$ in the given power series.}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Letting $a=0, \pm1, \pm2$, from \eqref{idofU (see \cite[Section 5]{AAT2 ), we obtain explicit formulas of the prefactor $\sum_{k\geq 0 b_k(a) q^k$ and the coefficient $c_{n (a,t)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Such formulas will be pivotal in our work.
elements.sent-2411-013: Our overarching goal in this work is to prove new Ramanujan--like congruences satisfied by the functions $MO(a,t;AN+B)$ for various values of $a$, $t$, $A$, and $B$, thus extending the set of known results that already appear in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-014: In the next section, we provide a list of such known results along with some obvious congruence relationships between some of these functions.
elements.sent-2411-015: We then prove numerous new results in the subsequent sections which are based on the value of the parameter $a$.
elements.sent-2411-016: We begin by highlighting a relatively obvious set of congruence results.
elements.sent-2411-017: For any $t$, \begin{equation}U_t(-1, q) \equiv U_t(1,q) \pmod{2} \ \ \ and\ \ \  U_t(-2, q) \equiv U_t(0, q) \equiv U_t(2,q) \pmod{2}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}U_t(-1, q) \equiv U_t(1,q) \pmod{2} \ \ \ and\ \ \  U_t(-2, q) \equiv U_t(0, q) \equiv U_t(2,q) \pmod{2}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-019: For any $t$, \begin{equation}U_t(-1,q) \equiv U_t(2,q) \pmod{3} \ \ \ and\ \ \  U_t(1, q) \equiv U_t(-2, q) \pmod{3}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}U_t(-1,q) \equiv U_t(2,q) \pmod{3} \ \ \ and\ \ \  U_t(1, q) \equiv U_t(-2, q) \pmod{3}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-021: Next, we focus our attention on past results which already appear in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-022: The case $a=-2$ has been investigated in \cite{AAT1 and \cite{AOS .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: In \cite{AAT1 , Amdeberhan, Andrews, and Tauraso discovered that, for all $N\geq 0$, \begin{equation}MO(-2,2;5N+2)\equiv 0\pmod 5 MO(-2,3;7N+3)\equiv MO(-2,3;7N+5)\equiv 0\pmod 7.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-024: They also conjectured that, for all $N\geq 0$, \begin{equation}\label{AATConjecture} MO(-2,10; 11N+7)\equiv 0\pmod{11}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-025: This has since been confirmed by Amdeberhan, Ono, and Singh \cite[Theorem 1.5]{AOS .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Morever, in \cite[Theorem 1.6]{AOS , the authors showed that, for all $J\geq 0$ and $N\geq 0$, \begin{equation}MO(-2,3J+2;3N+1)\equiv MO(-2,3J+2;3N+2)\equiv 0\pmod{3}, MO(-2,11J+10; 11N+7)\equiv 0\pmod{11}, MO(-2,17J+16; 17N+15)\equiv 0\pmod{17}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Amdeberhan, Andrews, and Tauraso also considered arithmetic results for other values of $a$ besides $-2$.
elements.sent-2411-028: As an example, they proved \cite[Theorem 2.2]{AAT2 that, for all $N\geq 0$ and for all $t$, \begin{equation}\label{1_t_3n2} MO(1,t;3N+2)=0.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Moreover, by \cite[Theorem 2.2]{AAT2 , for all $N\geq 0$, \begin{equation}MO(1,3;3N+1) \equiv 0 \pmod{3}.\label{1_3_3n1}\end{equation} With this set of results in hand, we now move ahead to prove additional infinite families of congruences satisfied by these functions.}
elements.sent-2411-030: From the table given in the introduction we have that \begin{equation}\sum_{k\geq 0} b_k(1) q^k=\frac{1}{f_3}=\sum_{k\geq 0} p(k) q^{3k}\end{equation} where $p(k)$ is the number of integer partitions of $k$.
elements.sent-2411-032: We already noted that $MO(1, t; 3N+2) = 0$ identically for all $t$.
elements.sent-2411-033: Next, we significantly extend the congruence in \eqref{1_3_3n1 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Since by \eqref{idp1 , \begin{equation}MO(1,t;3N+1)=\sum_{3k+\binom{n+1}2=3N+1}p(k)\cdot c_n(1,t),\end{equation} it suffices to check the case when $\binom{n+1 {2}\equiv 1\pmod3$, that is $n=3m+1$ for some integer $m$, and show that \begin{equation}c_{3m+1}(1,t)=\sum_{k=t}^{3m+1} (-1)^{3m+k+1}\left(\binom{3m+k+2}{2k+1}+\binom{3m+k+1}{2k+1}\right)\binom{k}{t} 3^{k-t}\end{equation} is divisible by $3$.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Actually, each summand is immediately divisible by $3$ due to the term $3^{k-t}$ when $k>t$.
elements.sent-2411-037: For $k=t$, replacing this value to compute the corresponding term, we obtain \begin{equation}(-1)^{n+t} \left(\binom{3m+t+2}{2t+1}+\binom{3m+t+1}{2t+1}\right)\binom{t}{t} 3^{t-t} = (-1)^{n+t}\frac{3(2m+1)}{2t+1}\binom{3m+t+1}{2t}\end{equation} which is divisible by $3$ as long as $2t+1\not \equiv 0\pmod 3$, that is $t \not \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$.. Our result follows.
elements.sent-2411-038: The next result provides a natural strengthening of Theorem \ref{MO3n+1v2 by increasing the modulus from 3 to 9.}
elements.sent-2411-040: As in the previous proof, by \eqref{idp1 , it suffices to show that \begin{equation}c_{3m+1}(1,t)=\sum_{k=t}^{3m+1} (-1)^{n+k}\left(\binom{3m+k+2}{2k+1}+\binom{3m+k+1}{2k+1}\right)\binom{k}{t} 3^{k-t}\end{equation} is divisible by $9$ when $t=3J+2$.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Each summand \begin{equation}(-1)^{n+k} \left(\binom{3m+k+2}{2k+1}+\binom{3m+k+1}{2k+1}\right)\binom{k}{t} 3^{k-t} = (-1)^{n+k}\frac{3(2m+1)}{2k+1}\binom{3m+k+1}{2k} \binom{k}{t} 3^{k-t}\end{equation} is immediately divisible by $9$ due to the term $3^{k-t}$ for $k>t+1$.
elements.sent-2411-042: Let us consider the cases $k=t$ and $k=t+1$.
elements.sent-2411-045: Combining all of the above facts yields our result.
elements.sent-2411-046: The next result is related to the well-known congruences of Ramanujan \cite{R21 : For all $N\geq 0$, \begin{equation}p(5N+4) \equiv 0 \pmod{5}, p(7N+5) \equiv 0 \pmod{7}, p(11N+6) \equiv 0 \pmod{11}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-047: For all $J\geq 0$ and all $N\geq 0$, \begin{equation}MO(1,5J+4;5N+2) \equiv 0 \pmod{5}, MO(1,7J+6;7N+1) \equiv 0 \pmod{7}, MO(1,11J+10;11N+7) \equiv 0 \pmod{11}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-049: The other two follow in a similar way.
elements.sent-2411-050: If $t=5J+4$ then, by Lucas's theorem (see for instance \cite{Fine ), $\binom{k {t}\equiv 0\pmod 5$ when $k\not \equiv 4\pmod 5$.}}

elements.sent-2411-053: Thus by \eqref{idp1 , \begin{equation}MO(1,5J+4;5N+2)=\sum_{3k+\binom{n+1}2=5N+2}p(k)\cdot c_n(1,5J+4) \equiv 0 \pmod{5}\end{equation} because when $\binom{n+1 2$ is divisible by $5$ then $3k\equiv 2\pmod{5}$, that is $k\equiv 4\pmod{5}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: In that case, $p(k)\equiv 0\pmod{5}$ by the first of Ramanujan's congruences recalled above.
elements.sent-2411-055: From the table given in the introduction we have that \begin{equation}\sum_{k\geq 0} b_k(-1) q^k=\frac{f_2 f_3}{f_1^2 f_6}=\sum_{k\geq 0} \B3(k) q^{k}\end{equation} where $\B3(k)$ is the number of almost $3$-regular overpartitions of $k$ \cite{Ballantine-Merca .}
elements.sent-2411-057: It is shown in \cite[Corollary 1.5]{Ballantine-Merca , that for all $k\geq 0$, \begin{equation}\B3(3k+1)\equiv 0 \pmod{2}\qquadand\qquad \B3(3k+2)\equiv 0 \pmod{4}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Moreover, by the proof given in the Appendix at the end of the paper, for all $k\geq 0$, \begin{equation}\B3(15k+7)\equiv 0\pmod{5}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-059: Equipped with these facts, we now proceed to prove several new arithmetic properties satisfied in the case when $a=-1$.

elements.sent-2411-065: Indeed, the last congruence holds due to the fact that \begin{equation}\frac{z^{2^J-2}(1+z)}{(1-z+z^2)^{2^J-1}} =\frac{z^{2^J-2}(1+z)^{2^J}(1+z^3)}{(1+z^3)^{2^J}} \equiv \frac{z^{2^J-2}(1+z^{2^J})(1+z^3)}{1+z^{3\cdot 2^J}} \pmod{2} \equiv \frac{z^{2^J-2}+z^{2^j+1}+z^{2^{J+1}-2}+z^{2^{J+1}+1}}{1+z^{3\cdot 2^J}} \pmod{2}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-129: Thanks to Alladi's leadership and inclusive spirit, the mathematics of Srinivasa Ramanujan has flourished within our community.
elements.sent-2411-130: In this paper, we prove several new infinite families of Ramanujan--like congruences satisfied by the coefficients of the generating function $U_t(a, q)$ which is an extension of MacMahon's generalized sum-of-divisors function.
elements.sent-2411-131: In \cite{AAT2 , Amdeberhan, Andrews, and the second author introduced the generating function \begin{equation}\label{defofU} U_t(a, q):=\sum_{1\leq n_1<n_2<\cdots<n_t}\frac{q^{n_1+n_2+\cdots+n_t}}{\prod_{k=1}^t(1+aq^{n_k}+q^{2n_k})}=\sum_{n\geq0} MO(a, t; n)q^n.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-132: The particular case $U_t(-2, q)$ was initially studied by P.. A.
elements.sent-2411-133: MacMahon \cite{MacMahon as a generalization of the sum-of-divisors function, \begin{equation}U_1(-2, q)=\sum_{n\geq 1} \frac{q^n}{(1-q^n)^2}=\sum_{n\geq 1} \sigma(n) q^n.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-134: The function $U_t(-2, q)$ has received much attention in recent years; see \cite{AAT1, AOS, Andrews-Rose, OS for more details about the results that have already been proven.}
elements.sent-2411-135: In \cite{AAT2 , the authors investigated $U_t(a, q)$ for the other cases when the roots of $1+ax+x^2=0$ are also roots of unity.}
elements.sent-2411-136: As such, in this note we will continue the study of $U_t(a, q)$ for this set of values of $a$, focusing our attention on arithmetic properties satisfied by the coefficients $MO(a, t; n)$.
elements.sent-2411-137: From Theorem 2.1 and Appendix 2 in \cite{AAT2 , we have that $U_t(a, q)$ can be written as the product of two generating functions: \begin{equation}\label{idofU} U_t(a, q)=\sum_{k\geq 0} b_k(a) q^k \cdot \sum_{n\geq 0} c_{n}(a, t) q^{\binom{n+1}{2}}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\sum_{k\geq 0} b_k(a) q^k=\prod_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(1+aq^n+q^{2n})(1-q^n)}\end{equation} and \begin{equation}c_n(a, t)=(-1)^{n-t}[z^n]\frac{z^t(1+z)}{(1+az+z^2)^{t+1}}\end{equation} where $[z^n]\left( \sum_{k\geq 0 h_kq^k \right) = h_n$, the coefficient of $z^n$ in the given power series.}}
elements.formula-2411-096: \begin{equation}\label{idofU} U_t(a, q)=\sum_{k\geq 0} b_k(a) q^k \cdot \sum_{n\geq 0} c_{n}(a, t) q^{\binom{n+1}{2}}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-138: Letting $a=0, \pm1, \pm2$, from \eqref{idofU (see \cite[Section 5]{AAT2 ), we obtain explicit formulas of the prefactor $\sum_{k\geq 0 b_k(a) q^k$ and the coefficient $c_{n (a, t)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-139: Our overarching goal in this work is to prove new Ramanujan--like congruences satisfied by the functions $MO(a, t; AN+B)$ for various values of $a$, $t$, $A$, and $B$, thus extending the set of known results that already appear in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-140: For any $t$, \begin{equation}U_t(-1, q) \equiv U_t(1, q) \pmod{2} \ \ \ and\ \ \  U_t(-2, q) \equiv U_t(0, q) \equiv U_t(2, q) \pmod{2}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-098: \begin{equation}U_t(-1, q) \equiv U_t(1, q) \pmod{2} \ \ \ and\ \ \  U_t(-2, q) \equiv U_t(0, q) \equiv U_t(2, q) \pmod{2}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-141: For any $t$, \begin{equation}U_t(-1, q) \equiv U_t(2, q) \pmod{3} \ \ \ and\ \ \  U_t(1, q) \equiv U_t(-2, q) \pmod{3}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-099: \begin{equation}U_t(-1, q) \equiv U_t(2, q) \pmod{3} \ \ \ and\ \ \  U_t(1, q) \equiv U_t(-2, q) \pmod{3}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-142: In \cite{AAT1 , Amdeberhan, Andrews, and the second author discovered that, for all $N\geq 0$, \begin{equation}MO(-2, 2; 5N+2)\equiv 0\pmod 5 MO(-2, 3; 7N+3)\equiv MO(-2, 3; 7N+5)\equiv 0\pmod 7.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-143: They also conjectured that, for all $N\geq 0$, \begin{equation}\label{AATConjecture} MO(-2, 10; 11N+7)\equiv 0\pmod{11}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-144: Morever, in \cite[Theorem 1.6]{AOS , the authors showed that, for all $J\geq 0$ and $N\geq 0$, \begin{equation}MO(-2, 3J+2; 3N+1)\equiv MO(-2, 3J+2; 3N+2)\equiv 0\pmod{3}, MO(-2, 11J+10; 11N+7)\equiv 0\pmod{11}, MO(-2, 17J+16; 17N+15)\equiv 0\pmod{17}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-145: Amdeberhan, Andrews, and the second author also considered arithmetic results for other values of $a$ besides $-2$.
elements.sent-2411-146: As an example, they proved \cite[Theorem 2.2]{AAT2 that, for all $N\geq 0$ and for all $t$, \begin{equation}\label{1_t_3n2} MO(1, t; 3N+2)=0.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-147: Moreover, by \cite[Theorem 2.2]{AAT2 , for all $N\geq 0$, \begin{equation}MO(1, 3; 3N+1) \equiv 0 \pmod{3}.\label{1_3_3n1}\end{equation} With this set of results in hand, we now move ahead to prove additional infinite families of congruences satisfied by these functions.}
